title: meeting notes workflow compiler
summary: A successful workflow experiment that turned long meeting transcripts into structured notes, actions, risks, and external-safe summaries.
narrative_origin: local workflow automation source
points: the experiment worked because it compressed a recurring meeting chore into a repeatable workflow
the useful system was not only summarization; it was preprocessing, chunking, staged prompts, and a final consolidated artifact
the source tree is not public-safe as-is because transcripts, generated notes, names, and legacy credential handling remain private
source_context: local source review; transcript bodies and generated meeting artifacts were treated as private evidence

title: adaptive oee reporting workbench
summary: A build log for a Python operations-reporting workbench with SQL query packs, Qt screens, adaptive OEE bands, anomaly models, and report-worker plumbing.
narrative_origin: daily production reporting that grew into analysis, thresholds, and model artifacts
points: reporting tools grow complicated when they must explain both metrics and exceptions
SQL query packs are a useful boundary between UI clicks and operational truth
adaptive threshold models are publishable as a pattern, but their artifacts can encode private context
refactoring a monolithic app is evidence when the new modules compile
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered Python reporting source: Qt UI modules, report worker, SQL queries, configuration layer, database workers, adaptive threshold and anomaly modules, model artifacts, refactor notes, and smoke scripts; Python syntax compilation passed on 2026-06-23; runtime tests were not run because they depend on GUI/database context

title: agent runbook workspace for machine reports
summary: A build log for a filesystem workspace that routes agents through machine-reporting runbooks, evidence capture, reusable scripts, and report-to-PDF assembly without loading the whole system at once.
narrative_origin: machine reports that needed repeatable agent context, not another loose prompt
points: an agent workspace is useful only if it routes context, tools, and artifacts instead of loading everything
reporting workflows need evidence capture before narrative interpretation
outputs should be dated artifacts, while reusable helpers belong in tools
fixture checks matter because report rendering can fail after the text looks correct
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered agent-workspace source: workspace model, routing policy, reporting workflow files, reusable scripts, query pack, report/PDF skill, and fixture verifier; PDF fixture smoke test passed on 2026-06-23

title: desktop certificate and audit PDF generator
summary: A build log for a Windows desktop tool that turns calibration, audit, and service-work models into previewable PDFs with templates, local job history, and repeatable numbering.
narrative_origin: field paperwork that needed to become repeatable documents without becoming a server
points: desktop document generation is mostly state management around the renderer
templates, defaults, numbering, preview, export, and job history all carry operational meaning
local SQLite history makes repeat work recoverable, but also creates a privacy boundary
brand assets and sample certificates are more identifying than the rendering code
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered desktop-document source: templates, renderer, template registry, form pages, local job store, refactor notes, and project files; source build passed with warnings on 2026-06-23; no source test project was present

title: edge log agent and central processor
summary: A build log for a two-part machine log collection system: an edge watcher that batches encoded records and a central processor that ingests, caches, stores, and serves them.
narrative_origin: machine log files that needed to leave the edge without losing provenance
points: edge collection is a contract between file changes, parsing, batching, and transport
central ingestion needs separate network, decoding, storage, cache, and API responsibilities
MessagePack can reduce payload size, but it also raises decode/versioning questions
topic names, customer ids, machine ids, and log paths are private operational context
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered log-collection source: edge watcher, parser, sender, encoder, server network/ingestion/database/cache/API modules, SQL setup, Docker files, and configuration docs; Python syntax compilation passed on 2026-06-23; runtime execution was not run because it requires log directories, broker/server services, and database infrastructure

title: finished-batch packout report export
summary: A build log for a Windows-first reporting tool that turns finished production batches into packout PDFs and CSVs, with a ledger so exports are not repeated.
narrative_origin: finished batches that still needed paperwork
points: the useful unit was not 'render a report'; it was 'turn a finished batch into one durable artifact'
an export ledger made the worker safe to rerun because success, failure, and retry state were remembered
the hard part was translating machine buckets and weights into the exact categories operators expect to see
a local service plus desktop app kept the workflow close to the machine without adding a server
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered reporting source: README, operations docs, release docs, git history, service code, and tests

title: local timesheet reporting workbench
summary: A build log for a local timesheet app that joins time entries to customers, projects, locations, and task types, then turns the record into weekly views and PDF/Excel exports.
narrative_origin: local time entries that needed to become reviewable weekly records
points: timesheet records become more useful when they are joined to operational reference data
PDF and spreadsheet exports turn local time entry into something reviewable outside the app
a committed local database is a privacy boundary, even when the code around it is harmless
verification can be mixed: a frontend build may pass while tests and backend install hygiene still fail
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered timesheet source: React/Redux frontend, Express/Sequelize/SQLite backend, reference-data routes, calendar editing, PDF/Excel export utilities, and roadmap notes; frontend production build passed on 2026-06-23, frontend formatter tests passed but the full Jest run failed on an Axios ESM transform issue, and backend npm ci failed because package and lockfile were out of sync

title: machine capture to training dataset
summary: A build log for turning opaque multi-channel machine capture archives into decoded previews, annotations, and reproducible training dataset exports.
narrative_origin: machine image captures that were visible only after decoding the archive format
points: the breakthrough was treating the capture file as a container with metadata and validated image payloads, not as an unknowable binary
payload length matching width times height times channel count made strict import possible
decoding was only the first half; the useful tool needed labels, review states, channel switching, and deterministic dataset export
sample archives, local paths, and generated preview images are privacy boundaries in machine-vision work
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered capture-decoder source: design docs, legacy Python probes, Go parser/decoder/exporter, React annotation UI, installer notes, and tests; backend and frontend tests passed on 2026-06-23

title: machine event timeline graph viewer
summary: A build log for a small Flask viewer that turns machine event spans into selectable dates, timeline overlays, and daily production summaries.
narrative_origin: machine event logs that needed a day-level visual debugger
points: timeline viewers become useful when they preserve the difference between running, idle, stopped, and break time
date selection is not just navigation; it is a way to ask which days have inspectable evidence
committed local databases are a privacy warning even when the code itself is publishable
a graph prototype can teach the report layer what summaries operators actually need
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered machine-event visualization source: Flask routes, SQLAlchemy models, migration files, import/cleanup scripts, browser timeline UI, and static graph helpers; Python syntax compilation passed on 2026-06-23; no source test suite was present

title: machine metrics collector with commissioning gates
summary: A build log for a Windows service and CLI that polls a machine API, writes time-series metrics, and refuses full ingestion until commissioning checks are satisfied.
narrative_origin: a machine feed that needed operational guardrails before it could become trusted data
points: a collector is not only a polling loop; it is configuration, schema ownership, retries, health, and commissioning state
ingestion should be gated until the machine endpoint, database, thresholds, and mapping assumptions are known
single authoritative SQL definition files can be safer than drifting migration fragments for this kind of operational tool
heartbeat output turns a background service into something another tool can supervise
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered machine-collector source: README, design guide, service runner, SQL definition runner, commissioning service, discovery/schema parser tests, and database policy notes; core project build passed on 2026-06-23; test project build was blocked by a test-framework reference mismatch in the isolated clone

title: mailbox graph helper functions
summary: A build log for a small Python helper layer around Microsoft Graph mailbox operations: device-code auth, message reads, send-mail, folder creation, and message moves.
narrative_origin: mailbox actions that needed a repeatable script boundary instead of manual clicking
points: mailbox automation is mostly auth boundary, permission scope, and careful message selection
helper functions should keep tokens, recipients, message ids, and folder ids out of logs
device-code auth is useful for local tools but should not be confused with service-grade auth
credential files removed from the current tree can still remain a repository-history risk
source_context: sanitized inspection of registered Python mail-helper sources: auth module, mailbox function module, setup runner, README, requirements, current tree scan, git history summary, mocked unit tests, and a thinner sibling source with draft-send/direct-send helpers; Python syntax compilation and two mocked unit tests passed on 2026-06-23 for the main helper, the sibling helper syntax-compiled but had no substantive tests, and live auth/mailbox execution was not run because it would require interactive account access and real mailbox operations

title: manual label printing from templates
summary: A build log for a Windows label-printing utility that turns bundled report templates, saved field values, presets, and layout nudges into repeatable manual print jobs.
narrative_origin: manual labels that needed remembered setup, not ad hoc typing
points: manual printing still needs a system when the same labels are recreated under pressure
the template file became the contract: supported items load, unsupported items fail visibly
saved values, presets, and layout nudges are the real operator surface around the print button
sample labels and template filenames are privacy boundaries because they often carry operational identity
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered label-printing source: README, template loader, renderer, storage layer, installer scripts, and tests; source tests passed on 2026-06-23

title: minute-level oee kpi runner
summary: A build log for a compact Python collector and KPI runner that polls machine endpoints, stores raw metrics, and computes minute-level OEE facts.
narrative_origin: a dashboard idea reduced down to the minute-by-minute data pipeline it needed
points: OEE dashboards depend on a quiet minute-level fact pipeline
raw machine payloads and calculated KPIs need different tables and different trust rules
backfill and live streaming are two modes of the same calculation
logs and config files can reveal more operational context than the scripts themselves
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered Python KPI source: endpoint collector, schema, config file, KPI runner, backfill script, and committed run log; Python syntax compilation passed on 2026-06-23; runtime execution was not run because it requires machine endpoints and database access

title: multi-site reporter configuration shell
summary: A build log for a Windows MAUI reporter shell that organizes per-site configuration, site selection, wizard creation, and current-site state.
narrative_origin: a reporting app that needed site configuration before reports could be trusted
points: multi-site reporting starts with configuration boundaries, not charts
a site registry makes the current context explicit before any report is generated
connection strings and API keys belong behind a privacy boundary even in prototypes
build plans are useful evidence when they match code that now compiles
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered Windows MAUI reporting source: configuration library, site registry models, config loader, site manager, current-site state service, splash/site-selection/site-wizard/dashboard view models, views, and implementation plan; source restore/build passed with one XAML binding warning on 2026-06-23; no source test suite was present

title: real-time anomaly detection on a grading line
summary: A one-night build that watches a multi-lane grading line and flags, in real time, when a lane's output mix drifts — using a contingency-table expected value and no training data.
narrative_origin: a grading line that never said when it was drifting
points: real-time anomaly detection is possible with only the current data and arithmetic — no training set, no history
the baseline can just be 'what the rest of the machine is doing right now', via a contingency-table expected value
two gates beat one: alarm only when a deviation is both statistically surprising and practically large
the hard part was not detection; it was translating a statistical spike into a sentence the operator already uses

title: static report portal preview
summary: A build log for a static browser preview that demonstrates a report review-to-publish workflow without pretending local state is production security.
narrative_origin: a customer preview that needed to show the workflow without holding the production system
points: a static preview can prove the workflow shape, but it cannot prove production security
the useful demo state is the transition from review to published, because that is when the recipient can finally see the report
role-separated screens reduce presentation confusion even when the roles are only browser-local
the production boundary is explicit: managed auth, private storage, backend authorization, immutable audit, and safe notifications
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered static portal source: README, security docs, data model notes, browser service code, data fixture, commit history, and tests; source tests passed on 2026-06-23

title: survey api client wrapper
summary: A build log for a small Python wrapper around a survey-platform API: token headers, surveys, responses, collectors, pagination, and custom error handling.
narrative_origin: survey data that needed a scriptable API boundary before agents could use it
points: API wrappers should make side-effectful survey, response, and collector operations explicit
survey titles, response bodies, collector ids, and access tokens are publishing boundaries
pagination is part of the client contract, not a detail to leave to every caller
a package can import from source while still failing as an installable project
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered survey-API wrapper source: README, package metadata, auth/survey/response/collector modules, pagination utility, exception class, demo runner, workspace config, git history summary, and bundled vendor API PDF metadata; Python syntax compilation and source-tree import smoke passed on 2026-06-23 after installing requests in an isolated virtual environment, package install failed because Poetry metadata did not match an importable package folder, and live API execution was not run because it would require a real access token and survey account data

title: target audience role proxy
summary: A build log for a very early React/TypeScript user-proxy interface that selects an audience-identification workflow and forwards candidate roles toward a downstream agent.
narrative_origin: a small interface for turning human role guesses into agent input
points: a user-proxy screen can make the human-to-agent boundary explicit before any model is wired in
role-identification workflows need privacy discipline because role lists can encode real organizational context
a passing build does not mean the prototype is tested when the default CRA test is stale
early agent UI sketches should say what is stubbed instead of pretending a backend exists
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered React/TypeScript assistant-ui source: README, package manifest, App component, UserProxy component, CSS, default test, and git history summary; npm ci failed on a React Scripts / TypeScript peer dependency conflict, npm ci --legacy-peer-deps then production build passed on 2026-06-23, and the test run failed because the default CRA test still expected the removed starter link

title: workbook row to packout PDF
summary: A build log for a Windows reporting bridge that turns formatted Excel workbooks and selected ledger rows into one-page operational PDFs.
narrative_origin: a workbook that already held the report but not the handoff artifact
points: sometimes the fastest report renderer is the tool the workbook already lives in
a spreadsheet row can become a durable document only if the workbook layout is treated as a contract
the useful wrapper was not the PDF button; it was the row picker, validation, default filename, and installed Windows path
example workbooks and output names are privacy boundaries because operational labels travel through them
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered workbook-report source: README, Excel export script, row-based PDF generator, desktop wrapper, installer scripts, commit history, and tests; source tests passed on 2026-06-23

title: wpf operations console shell
summary: A build log for a WPF desktop shell that frames navigation, settings, theme control, and future machine/API configuration before the real workflow arrives.
narrative_origin: a desktop shell for operations tooling before the workflow hardened
points: a desktop app can be real infrastructure before it has real domain screens
navigation, hosting, settings, theming, and service boundaries are the first durable decisions
starter UI labels and stub machine fields are useful evidence, but not publishable identity
a shell build should be labeled as a shell, not inflated into an application
source_context: sanitized inspection of a registered WPF desktop source: solution/project files, host service, navigation window, pages, view models, settings scaffold, assets, and task metadata; source restore/build passed with nullable warnings on 2026-06-23; no source test suite was present

title: dashboard without decisions
summary: A graveyard note for a dashboard idea that displayed activity but did not help anyone decide what to do next.
narrative_origin: discarded build
points: a dashboard that shows activity but changes no decision is not operationally useful
visibility is weaker than consequence
the useful remnant was the question of what would make a display decisive

title: data trust ladder
summary: A static experiment for separating raw data, clean data, trusted data, explanation, and action.
narrative_origin: dashboard notes
points: raw data, clean data, trusted data, explanation, and action are different states
a dashboard can look useful while still being stuck at the first rung
the point is to ask which step fails before adding more instrumentation

title: operator knowledge as signal
summary: A pattern for treating experienced human observation as a system signal rather than an anecdote outside the model.
narrative_origin: field notes
points: operator knowledge is a noisy but real sensor layer
experienced behavior changes can warn before dashboards do
the goal is to preserve the signal without pretending it is flawless

title: static archive scaffold
summary: A build log for turning a folder of records into a static archive with routes, metadata, and readable MDX pages.
narrative_origin: build bench
points: local MDX records became the source for routes, lists, metadata, and related links
static export keeps the archive public without adding a server runtime
simple validation is enough for the first archive, but it is not a full editorial report

title: the operator knows before the database does
summary: A field note about human observation as a richer signal set than the data model sometimes captures.
narrative_origin: machine room
points: operators often notice state changes before formal data models do
human observation can be evidence without being treated as perfect truth
the missing signal is often contextual, not absent
source_context: anonymized production floor observation

title: why this exists
summary: The opening record for an anonymous archive of systems, tools, fragments, and things understood later.
narrative_origin: personal archive
points: the archive is a record of unfinished systems, not a portfolio
useful work disappears unless it leaves a trail
anonymity keeps the attention on the work instead of the author

title: you pay for the problem that disappears
summary: A short note about valuing solved problems by the consequence they remove, not by visible effort.
narrative_origin: private note
points: the value of work is the consequence removed, not the effort displayed
the best solved problems can become invisible after they disappear
